description: Coordinate concurrent work safely across local threads, async runtimes, and distributed systems.
tags: Coordinate concurrent work safely across local threads, async runtimes, and distributed systems

solutions.name: Mutex
solutions.blurb: Mutual-exclusion lock for protecting shared state from concurrent access.

solutions.name: Go Routines + Channels
solutions.blurb: Lightweight goroutines communicate through typed channels for structured concurrent work.

solutions.name: async/await
solutions.blurb: Promise-based syntax for composing asynchronous operations without callback nesting.

solutions.name: Leader Election
solutions.blurb: Selects one coordinator among distributed peers to serialize decisions or ownership.

solutions.name: Distributed Locking
solutions.blurb: Uses a shared coordination service to prevent duplicate distributed work.

solutions.name: Redlock
solutions.blurb: Redis-based distributed lock algorithm for coordinating clients across independent Redis nodes.

solutions.name: Java Concurrency
solutions.blurb: Java primitives and executors for locks, atomics, thread pools, and concurrent collections.

solutions.name: Go routines
solutions.blurb: Goroutines and channels model concurrent execution and communication in Go.

solutions.name: Node async
solutions.blurb: Event-loop based asynchronous I/O for high-concurrency server workflows.

solutions.name: Threading
solutions.blurb: Runs work concurrently in threads that may share memory and require coordination.

solutions.name: mutex
solutions.blurb: Mutual-exclusion lock for protecting shared state from concurrent access.

solutions.name: actor model
solutions.blurb: Models concurrent systems as isolated actors that communicate by messages.

avoidWhen: A queue or database transaction would serialize the work more simply
The workload is mostly sequential
tradeoffs: Higher throughput can add nondeterminism
Debugging and testing require disciplined synchronization
firstMove: Start by removing shared mutable state; use queues, actors, or transactions before low-level locks when possible.

solutions.name: MapReduce
solutions.blurb: Splits large batch jobs into map and reduce phases over distributed data.

solutions.name: ETL jobs
solutions.blurb: Extracts, transforms, and loads data between operational systems and analytics stores.

solutions.name: Apache Airflow
solutions.blurb: Orchestrates scheduled data pipelines as Python-defined DAGs.

solutions.name: Apache Spark
solutions.blurb: Distributed engine for large-scale batch processing, SQL, streaming, and ML workloads.

solutions.name: Pandas
solutions.blurb: In-memory data analysis and transformation library for tabular data.

solutions.name: dbt
solutions.blurb: Builds versioned, tested analytics transformations inside the data warehouse.

solutions.name: ETL
solutions.blurb: Extracts data, transforms it before loading, then writes curated data into the target system.

solutions.name: ELT
solutions.blurb: Extracts and loads raw data first, then transforms it inside the warehouse or lakehouse.

solutions.name: Reverse ETL
solutions.blurb: Moves modeled warehouse data back into operational tools such as CRMs, support systems, or ad platforms.

avoidWhen: Users need low-latency streaming updates
The dataset is small enough for a direct query
tradeoffs: Batch processing is reliable and simple but adds freshness delay
Orchestration reduces manual work but adds scheduling failure modes
ETL centralizes transformation before loading; ELT favors raw landing plus warehouse-native transformation
scale: Files to data lakes and warehouses
firstMove: Start with freshness, ownership, and whether transforms belong before or after loading; choose ETL/ELT tooling after the data contract is clear.

description: Process events and data streams in real time or near real time with brokers, consumers, and pipelines.
tags: Process events and data streams in real time or near real time with brokers, consumers, and pipelines

solutions.name: Kafka
solutions.blurb: Distributed append-only log for event streaming, messaging, and stream processing.

solutions.name: Kinesis
solutions.blurb: AWS managed service for collecting, processing, and analyzing streaming data.

solutions.name: NATS
solutions.blurb: High-performance messaging system for pub/sub, request-reply, and streaming workloads.

solutions.name: Kafka
solutions.language: Java (official client; common clients in Python, Go, JS)
solutions.blurb: Distributed log for publish, subscribe, consumer groups, partitions, offsets, replay, and stream processing.
solutions.timeComplexity: O(1) per produced/consumed record plus broker, partition, and replication cost
solutions.spaceComplexity: O(batch size) client memory; broker storage depends on retention and replication

solutions.name: RabbitMQ
solutions.language: Python (Pika); broker supports AMQP clients in many languages
solutions.blurb: Queue-oriented broker for declare queue, publish, consume, ack, routing keys, exchanges, and pub/sub patterns.
solutions.timeComplexity: O(1) per publish/consume operation plus broker routing and durability cost
solutions.spaceComplexity: O(message size) client memory; broker queue storage grows with backlog and durability
solutions.code:
import pika

connection = pika.BlockingConnection(pika.ConnectionParameters(host="localhost"))
channel = connection.channel()

# Declare is idempotent: safe for producer and consumer startup.
channel.queue_declare(queue="orders", durable=True)

# Publish to the default exchange using the queue name as routing key.
channel.basic_publish(
    exchange="",
    routing_key="orders",
    body=b"order-1 created",
    properties=pika.BasicProperties(delivery_mode=2),
)

def handle_message(ch, method, properties, body):
    print(body.decode())
    ch.basic_ack(delivery_tag=method.delivery_tag)

channel.basic_consume(queue="orders", on_message_callback=handle_message)
channel.start_consuming()

solutions.name: Kinesis
solutions.blurb: Managed AWS stream for create/list streams, put records, read shards, checkpoints, retention, and enhanced consumers.
solutions.timeComplexity: O(1) per put_record/get_records call; throughput is bounded by shard limits
solutions.spaceComplexity: O(batch size) client memory; service storage depends on stream retention
solutions.code:
import boto3

kinesis = boto3.client("kinesis", region_name="us-east-1")
stream = "orders"

# Write one event. Use put_records for batching in production.
kinesis.put_record(
    StreamName=stream,
    PartitionKey="order-1",
    Data=b"order-1 created",
)

# Read from a shard. Real consumers checkpoint and loop with backoff.
shards = kinesis.list_shards(StreamName=stream)["Shards"]
shard_id = shards[0]["ShardId"]
iterator = kinesis.get_shard_iterator(
    StreamName=stream,
    ShardId=shard_id,
    ShardIteratorType="TRIM_HORIZON",
)["ShardIterator"]

records = kinesis.get_records(ShardIterator=iterator, Limit=10)
for record in records["Records"]:
    print(record["PartitionKey"], record["Data"].decode())

solutions.name: NATS
solutions.language: Go (server/client); clients available for many languages
solutions.blurb: Subject-based messaging for connect, publish, subscribe, queue groups, request-reply, and optional JetStream persistence.
solutions.timeComplexity: O(1) per publish/receive operation plus broker routing and subscriber fanout
solutions.spaceComplexity: O(message size) client memory; JetStream storage depends on retention and acknowledgements

solutions.name: Kafka consumers
solutions.blurb: Consumers subscribe to Kafka topics and process records from assigned partitions.

solutions.name: log processing
solutions.blurb: Consumes logs as event streams for indexing, monitoring, alerting, or analytics.

avoidWhen: Hourly or daily freshness is enough
Ordering and replay semantics are not understood
tradeoffs: Low latency and decoupling come with delivery, ordering, and replay complexity
Consumers must handle duplicates and backpressure
firstMove: Start with event contracts, ordering, replay, idempotency, and consumer failure handling before choosing Kafka, Kinesis, RabbitMQ, or NATS.

solutions.name: Redis
solutions.blurb: Low-latency in-memory data store commonly used for caching, queues, and counters.
solutions.code:
import redis

client = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
key = 'profile:42'
profile = client.get(key)
if profile is None:
    profile = load_profile_from_database(42)
    client.setex(key, 300, profile)
return profile
solutions.timeComplexity: GET/SET are O(1) average for string keys
solutions.spaceComplexity: O(number of cached keys and values)

solutions.name: Memcached
solutions.blurb: Simple distributed memory cache for reducing database and service load.

solutions.name: CDN
solutions.blurb: Caches static and dynamic content close to users to reduce latency and origin load.

avoidWhen: Data must always be strongly fresh
Cache invalidation rules are unclear
tradeoffs: Caches reduce latency and load but introduce staleness and invalidation complexity
Operational wins depend on hit rate
firstMove: Start with the invalidation model and TTL policy; add Redis or application caches only after the source of truth and staleness tolerance are clear.

description: Find valid assignments that satisfy logical, combinatorial, or optimization constraints.
tags: Find valid assignments that satisfy logical, combinatorial, or optimization constraints

solutions.name: Z3
solutions.blurb: SMT solver for logical formulas over arithmetic, bit-vectors, arrays, and more.

solutions.name: Gurobi
solutions.blurb: Commercial solver for linear, integer, quadratic, and constrained optimization models.

solutions.name: OR-Tools CP-SAT
solutions.blurb: CP-SAT solver for combinatorial optimization, scheduling, and assignment problems.
solutions.code:
from ortools.sat.python import cp_model

model = cp_model.CpModel()
x = model.new_int_var(0, 10, 'x')
y = model.new_int_var(0, 10, 'y')
model.add(x + 2 * y <= 14)
model.maximize(3 * x + 4 * y)
solver = cp_model.CpSolver()
status = solver.solve(model)
if status in (cp_model.OPTIMAL, cp_model.FEASIBLE):
    print(solver.value(x), solver.value(y))
solutions.timeComplexity: NP-hard search; solver time depends on constraints and pruning

solutions.name: MiniZinc

solutions.name: Sudoku
solutions.blurb: Classic constraint satisfaction puzzle involving all-different row, column, and box constraints.

solutions.name: scheduling
solutions.blurb: Assigns work, time, or resources while respecting constraints and preferences.
solutions.timeComplexity: NP-hard for general scheduling; CP-SAT search is model dependent

solutions.name: Prolog-style reasoning

avoidWhen: A direct formula or greedy rule solves the case

firstMove: Start by expressing variables, domains, and constraints declaratively; use CP-SAT or SAT/SMT before writing brittle search logic.

solutions.name: Grafana

solutions.name: ELK

solutions.name: OpenTelemetry

solutions.name: Zookeeper

solutions.name: etcd
solutions.blurb: Consistent distributed key-value store used for configuration and coordination.

solutions.name: Consul

avoidWhen: A database row lock or queue is enough
The coordination service would become a new critical dependency without need
tradeoffs: Coordination services simplify distributed state but add quorum and availability concerns
Clients must handle session and lease failures
firstMove: Start by deciding whether you need messaging, coordination, or consensus; avoid adding quorum systems when a queue or database lock is enough.

description: Find path to goal through defined state transitions

solutions.name: Pathfinding
solutions.blurb: Finds a path through a state space or graph from a start state to a goal.
solutions.timeComplexity: O(V + E) for unweighted BFS shortest path

solutions.name: game AI
solutions.blurb: Applies search, planning, and decision algorithms to game agents and strategy.

solutions.name: puzzle solvers
solutions.blurb: Explores legal moves or states to find a valid or optimal puzzle solution.

avoidWhen: The state space is tiny enough for direct enumeration
There is no clear successor function or goal test
tradeoffs: Search is general but can explode combinatorially
Pruning and heuristics often determine practicality
scale: Small puzzles to large planning spaces

description: Use heuristics to guide search faster

solutions.name: A*
solutions.blurb: Best-first graph search that combines path cost and heuristic estimate.
solutions.timeComplexity: Worst-case O(E); often much faster with an admissible heuristic

solutions.name: IDA*
solutions.blurb: Iterative-deepening A* reduces memory use while using a heuristic cost bound.
solutions.timeComplexity: O(b^d) worst case; heuristic quality dominates

solutions.name: beam search
solutions.blurb: Keeps only the best candidate states at each depth to control search width.
solutions.timeComplexity: O(depth * width * branching factor)

avoidWhen: No useful heuristic exists
Optimality guarantees matter and the heuristic is not admissible
tradeoffs: Heuristics speed search but can bias or miss good solutions
Memory use can grow quickly

description: Maximize or minimize under constraints

solutions.name: Supply chain optimization
solutions.blurb: Optimizes sourcing, production, inventory, routing, or allocation under constraints.

solutions.name: scheduling
solutions.blurb: Assigns work, time, or resources while respecting constraints and preferences.
solutions.code:
from ortools.sat.python import cp_model

model = cp_model.CpModel()
starts = [model.new_int_var(0, 20, f'start_{i}') for i in range(3)]
durations = [3, 4, 2]
intervals = [model.new_interval_var(starts[i], durations[i], starts[i] + durations[i], f'task_{i}') for i in range(3)]
model.add_no_overlap(intervals)
model.minimize(max(starts[i] + durations[i] for i in range(3)))
solver = cp_model.CpSolver()
solver.solve(model)
print([solver.value(start) for start in starts])
solutions.timeComplexity: NP-hard for general scheduling; CP-SAT search is model dependent

avoidWhen: The relationships are highly nonlinear or hard to linearize
A simple rule gives good enough results
tradeoffs: Mathematical models give strong solutions but require precise formulation
Integer programs can become expensive at scale

description: Make local optimal choices

solutions.name: Huffman coding
solutions.blurb: Builds an optimal prefix code by repeatedly combining the least frequent symbols.
solutions.timeComplexity: O(n log n) for n distinct symbols

solutions.name: MST
solutions.blurb: Finds a minimum spanning tree connecting all graph vertices at minimum total edge weight.
solutions.timeComplexity: O(E log E) for Kruskal sorting

solutions.name: activity selection
solutions.blurb: Selects a maximum set of non-overlapping intervals by choosing earliest finishing activities.
solutions.timeComplexity: O(n log n) including sort; O(n) if already sorted by finish time

avoidWhen: A local best choice can block a better global solution
Proof of greedy choice property is missing
tradeoffs: Greedy methods are simple and fast but correctness is problem-specific
Often useful as heuristics even when not exact
scale: Small to very large algorithmic workloads
firstMove: Start by proving the greedy choice property or accepting approximation; otherwise use DP, search, or optimization solvers.

description: Break problem into overlapping subproblems

solutions.name: DP for knapsack
solutions.blurb: Uses subproblem tables to maximize value under a capacity constraint.
solutions.timeComplexity: O(n * capacity)

solutions.name: sequence alignment
solutions.blurb: Aligns sequences by optimizing match, mismatch, and gap costs.
solutions.timeComplexity: O(n * m)
solutions.spaceComplexity: O(n * m); can be O(min(n, m)) for score only

solutions.name: Edit distance
solutions.blurb: Computes the minimum insertions, deletions, and substitutions needed to transform one string into another.
solutions.timeComplexity: O(n * m)
solutions.spaceComplexity: O(n * m); can be O(min(n, m)) for distance only

avoidWhen: Subproblems do not repeat
State dimensions make memory or time explode
tradeoffs: DP gives exact answers for many structured problems but requires careful state design
Space optimization can make implementations harder to read
scale: Algorithmic problems with bounded state spaces
firstMove: Start by identifying state, recurrence, and ordering; prefer library/solver alternatives when the state space is generic optimization.

description: Optimizing flow in a network

solutions.name: Edmonds–Karp (max flow)
solutions.blurb: BFS-based augmenting paths (Ford–Fulkerson with BFS); O(V·E²).
solutions.code:
from collections import deque

def edmonds_karp(cap, s, t):
    # cap[u][v] = capacity; build residual graph on the fly
    n = len(cap)
    flow = 0
    parent = [-1]*n
    def bfs():
        for i in range(n): parent[i] = -1
        parent[s] = s
        q = deque([s])
        while q:
            u = q.popleft()
            for v in range(n):
                if parent[v] == -1 and cap[u][v] > 0:
                    parent[v] = u
                    if v == t: return True
                    q.append(v)
        return False
    while bfs():
        # find bottleneck
        v = t; bottleneck = float('inf')
        while v != s:
            u = parent[v]
            bottleneck = min(bottleneck, cap[u][v])
            v = u
        # augment
        v = t
        while v != s:
            u = parent[v]
            cap[u][v] -= bottleneck
            cap[v][u] += bottleneck
            v = u
        flow += bottleneck
    return flow

# Example usage:
# cap is an NxN matrix; s=0, t=N-1
# cap[u][v] = capacity from u to v; missing edges are 0

solutions.name: Dinic (max flow)
solutions.blurb: Level graph + blocking flow; O(E·V²) general, faster in practice.
solutions.timeComplexity: O(V^2 * E) general; faster on many unit-capacity graphs

solutions.name: Push–relabel (max flow)
solutions.blurb: Preflow with local pushes and relabels; near-linear on many sparse graphs.
solutions.timeComplexity: O(V^3) generic bound; often strong on sparse graphs

solutions.name: NetworkX maximum_flow
solutions.code:
import networkx as nx
G = nx.DiGraph()
G.add_edge('s','a', capacity=3)
G.add_edge('s','b', capacity=2)
G.add_edge('a','b', capacity=1)
G.add_edge('a','t', capacity=2)
G.add_edge('b','t', capacity=3)
flow_val, flow_dict = nx.maximum_flow(G, 's', 't')
print(flow_val)
solutions.blurb: NetworkX helper for computing maximum flow value and flow assignment in directed graphs.
solutions.timeComplexity: Depends on selected flow algorithm; default uses preflow-push style methods

solutions.name: OR-Tools SimpleMaxFlow
solutions.code:
from ortools.graph.python import max_flow
mf = max_flow.SimpleMaxFlow()
# add arcs: (tail, head, capacity)
mf.add_arc_with_capacity(0, 1, 3)
mf.add_arc_with_capacity(0, 2, 2)
mf.add_arc_with_capacity(1, 2, 1)
mf.add_arc_with_capacity(1, 3, 2)
mf.add_arc_with_capacity(2, 3, 3)
status = mf.solve(0, 3)
print('max flow =', mf.optimal_flow())
solutions.blurb: OR-Tools max-flow API for solving directed capacitated network flow problems.
solutions.timeComplexity: Strongly polynomial max-flow implementation; practical cost depends on graph size

solutions.name: Boost Graph (C++)
solutions.blurb: Use push_relabel_max_flow or edmonds_karp_max_flow for high-performance C++.

avoidWhen: The problem lacks conservation/capacity structure
A simpler shortest-path or matching formulation is enough
tradeoffs: Flow models are powerful and exact but require careful graph construction
Algorithm choice affects performance on dense or sparse networks
scale: Small graphs to large optimization networks

description: Declare what should be true

solutions.name: Prolog
solutions.blurb: Declarative language based on facts, rules, unification, and backtracking search.

solutions.name: SQL
solutions.blurb: Declarative language for querying and transforming relational data.

avoidWhen: Performance predictability is more important than declarative clarity
The team is unfamiliar with logic programming semantics
tradeoffs: Rules can be concise and expressive but debugging search behavior can be non-obvious
Works best when relationships are naturally logical
scale: Rule engines and symbolic reasoning systems

description: Data transformed by functions

solutions.name: Map/filter/reduce
solutions.blurb: Composes collection transformations with mapping, filtering, and reduction steps.

solutions.name: stream transformations
solutions.blurb: Processes data as chained transformations over streams or iterables.

avoidWhen: Step-by-step mutation is clearer
Debuggability suffers from overly dense chaining
tradeoffs: Pipelines improve composition and readability when kept small
Deep chains can hide intermediate state and errors
firstMove: Start with pure transforms and data contracts; use pipelines for composable transformations, not hidden orchestration state.

description: Agreement across distributed systems

solutions.name: Paxos
solutions.blurb: Family of consensus protocols for agreeing on values despite failures.

solutions.name: Raft
solutions.blurb: Leader-based consensus algorithm designed to be easier to understand than Paxos.

solutions.name: Zookeeper
solutions.blurb: Distributed coordination service for configuration, naming, leader election, and locks.

avoidWhen: Eventual consistency is acceptable
A managed coordination service already handles it
tradeoffs: Consensus provides strong correctness but adds latency and operational complexity
Failure modes require careful implementation or mature libraries

description: Prove program correctness

solutions.name: SAT solving
solutions.blurb: Determines whether a Boolean formula has an assignment that makes it true.

solutions.name: formal specs (Z, TLA+)
solutions.blurb: Mathematical specifications used to model and verify system behavior before implementation.

avoidWhen: The model would be more expensive than the risk
Requirements are not stable enough to specify
tradeoffs: Model checking finds subtle bugs but can face state explosion
Formal specs need maintenance as designs change

description: Model behavior of systems over time

solutions.name: Monte Carlo
solutions.blurb: Uses repeated random sampling to estimate outcomes, distributions, or expectations.

solutions.name: queue simulation
solutions.blurb: Models arrivals, service times, and waiting behavior in queueing systems.

avoidWhen: Exact formulas are available and trustworthy
Input distributions are unknown or misleading
tradeoffs: Simulation handles uncertainty and dynamics but depends on model assumptions
More runs improve confidence but cost time

subcategory: Language models, agents, RAG, structured outputs, and evaluation
description: Build language-model applications that reason over prompts, tools, context, retrieved data, and evaluation feedback.

solutions.name: RAG
solutions.blurb: Retrieves relevant external context before generation so answers can use private or changing data.

solutions.name: Agent tools / skills
solutions.blurb: Exposes bounded external actions or workflows that a model can call when plain text generation is not enough.

solutions.name: Structured outputs
solutions.blurb: Constrains model responses to a predictable schema for reliable downstream automation.

solutions.name: Prompt routing
solutions.blurb: Routes requests to specialized prompts, models, tools, or retrieval sources based on task shape.

solutions.name: Evaluation harnesses
solutions.blurb: Uses test sets, graders, traces, and regression checks to keep model behavior measurable.

solutions.name: Ollama
solutions.blurb: Runs open-weight language models locally with a simple model library and HTTP API.

solutions.name: llama.cpp
solutions.blurb: Efficient CPU/GPU inference runtime for GGUF-format transformer models.

solutions.name: vLLM
solutions.blurb: High-throughput serving engine for transformer inference with paged attention and OpenAI-compatible APIs.

avoidWhen: A deterministic parser or query is enough
No evaluation set exists for risky automation
tradeoffs: LLMs are flexible but nondeterministic
RAG improves freshness but depends on retrieval quality
Agents add capability and failure modes
scale: Local prototype to production AI service
firstMove: Start with task shape, evaluation set, and failure modes; choose prompting, tools, RAG, or structured outputs based on measurable behavior.

subcategory: Short-term context, retrieval memory, summaries, knowledge graphs, and agent state
description: Choose how an LLM application preserves useful context across turns, sessions, documents, and tools.

solutions.name: Context-window memory
solutions.blurb: Keeps recent conversation or state directly in the model context for maximum fidelity and minimum infrastructure.

solutions.name: Summary memory
solutions.blurb: Condenses older context into summaries to reduce token use while retaining broad state.

solutions.name: Vector retrieval memory
solutions.blurb: Stores chunks or memories as embeddings and retrieves semantically similar context when needed.

solutions.name: Knowledge graph memory
solutions.blurb: Stores entities and relationships explicitly so context can be queried by structure rather than similarity alone.

solutions.name: Episodic agent memory
solutions.blurb: Stores task episodes, outcomes, and preferences for future planning and personalization.

avoidWhen: Single-turn tasks
Strict privacy rules forbid persistence
The context fits directly in the prompt
tradeoffs: Longer memory improves continuity but can add stale or irrelevant context
Vector memory is flexible but can miss exact facts
Graph memory is precise but requires extraction and curation
scale: Single-user local apps to agent platforms
firstMove: Start by deciding what must persist, expire, be cited, or be forgotten; avoid vector memory until retrieval quality is measurable.

subcategory: Embedding indexes, semantic retrieval, and vector databases
description: Represent text, images, or records as vectors, then retrieve semantically similar items for search, RAG, recommendations, or memory.

solutions.name: FAISS
solutions.blurb: In-process similarity search library useful for local indexes and high-performance ANN experiments.

solutions.name: pgvector
solutions.blurb: Adds vector similarity search to PostgreSQL so embeddings live near relational data.

solutions.name: Qdrant
solutions.blurb: Vector database for semantic search with filtering, payloads, HNSW indexing, and production-oriented APIs.

solutions.name: Chroma
solutions.blurb: Developer-friendly embedding database often used for local RAG prototypes.

solutions.name: Weaviate
solutions.blurb: Vector database with hybrid search, schema, modules, and managed deployment options.

solutions.name: Milvus
solutions.blurb: Distributed vector database designed for large-scale similarity search workloads.

solutions.name: Chunking strategy
solutions.blurb: Splits documents into retrievable units; chunk size and overlap strongly affect recall and precision.

solutions.name: Hybrid search
solutions.blurb: Combines keyword and vector retrieval to improve exact-term matching and semantic recall.

solutions.name: Reranking
solutions.blurb: Reorders retrieved candidates with a stronger model to improve final context quality.

avoidWhen: Exact SQL predicates solve the problem
The corpus is tiny enough for direct scanning
Embedding privacy/cost constraints are unresolved
tradeoffs: Vector search improves semantic recall but can miss exact terms
Filtering and chunking matter as much as database choice
Operational complexity rises with corpus size and freshness needs
firstMove: Start with chunking, metadata filters, and retrieval evaluation; choose vector database only after recall/precision needs are testable.

subcategory: Fine-tuning, LoRA, QLoRA, adapters, and prompt/RAG alternatives
description: Choose how to adapt model behavior, knowledge, or style without confusing model classes with training techniques.

solutions.name: Full fine-tuning
solutions.blurb: Updates model weights for a target task or domain when data and evaluation justify the cost.

solutions.name: LoRA
solutions.blurb: Trains low-rank adapter matrices instead of all model weights, reducing storage and training cost.

solutions.name: QLoRA
solutions.blurb: Fine-tunes low-rank adapters on quantized base models to reduce memory requirements for adaptation work.

solutions.name: Adapters
solutions.blurb: Adds small trainable modules while keeping the base model mostly frozen.

solutions.name: Prompt tuning
solutions.blurb: Learns task-specific prompt embeddings rather than changing the full model.

solutions.name: RAG instead of fine-tuning
solutions.blurb: Keeps knowledge outside the model and retrieves it at runtime when freshness or attribution matters.

avoidWhen: The need is fresh factual knowledge better handled by RAG
No evaluation set exists
Inference-only prompting solves the problem
tradeoffs: Fine-tuning can improve behavior but adds data and evaluation burden
LoRA saves memory but still requires training discipline
RAG is easier to update but depends on retrieval quality
scale: Single adapter to model operations pipeline

subcategory: Diffusion models, image generation, LoRA style adapters, and control techniques
description: Generate or edit images and media with diffusion-style models and adaptation/control layers.

solutions.name: Stable Diffusion
solutions.blurb: Latent diffusion model family for text-to-image, image-to-image, inpainting, and related workflows.

solutions.name: Diffusers
solutions.blurb: Hugging Face library for running and composing diffusion model pipelines.

solutions.name: Image LoRA
solutions.blurb: Small adapter weights that steer image generation style, subject, or concept without replacing the base model.

solutions.name: ControlNet
solutions.blurb: Adds spatial control signals such as edges, poses, or depth maps to diffusion generation.

solutions.name: Inpainting
solutions.blurb: Regenerates masked regions while preserving the surrounding image context.

avoidWhen: The task is language reasoning or retrieval
Licensing/provenance constraints are unresolved
Pixel-perfect deterministic output is required
tradeoffs: Diffusion models are powerful but stochastic
Control techniques improve composition but add workflow complexity
LoRA is adaptation, not a base model family
scale: Local GPU workflows to hosted generation services

subcategory: V-JEPA, VL-JEPA, vision-language representations, and world-model style planning
description: Use self-supervised vision or vision-language representations when perception, temporal understanding, or planning matters more than text generation.

solutions.name: V-JEPA
solutions.blurb: Learns visual representations by predicting missing video features in embedding space rather than generating pixels.

solutions.name: V-JEPA 2
solutions.blurb: Meta world-model research that extends JEPA-style video representations toward visual understanding, prediction, physical reasoning, and robot planning.

solutions.name: VL-JEPA
solutions.blurb: Research-stage vision-language JEPA approach that predicts text embeddings rather than treating every vision-language task as token generation.

solutions.name: Vision-language models
solutions.blurb: Models that connect images or video with text for captioning, VQA, retrieval, and multimodal reasoning.

solutions.name: World models
solutions.blurb: Learn latent dynamics of environments for prediction, planning, simulation, or robotics.

avoidWhen: You need text generation or document Q&A
A standard classifier or detector solves the task
Research maturity risk is unacceptable
tradeoffs: JEPA-style models are representation learners, not LLMs or diffusion generators
They can support planning/perception but require task-specific integration
Research progress is fast and APIs are less standardized
scale: Research prototypes to specialized perception systems

subcategory: Model APIs, self-hosted inference, static apps, containers, serverless, and edge
description: Choose how AI systems, model endpoints, and supporting applications run, scale, observe, and ship.

solutions.name: Static hosting
solutions.blurb: Serves prebuilt assets from a CDN or static host with low operational overhead.

solutions.name: Containers
solutions.blurb: Packages app code and dependencies into portable images for consistent runtime environments.

solutions.name: Kubernetes
solutions.blurb: Schedules and manages containerized services across clusters with rollout and scaling controls.

solutions.name: Serverless functions
solutions.blurb: Runs small request or event handlers without managing servers directly.

solutions.name: Edge functions
solutions.blurb: Runs latency-sensitive logic near users at CDN edge locations.

solutions.name: Managed model API
solutions.blurb: Uses a provider-hosted model endpoint to avoid model infrastructure and focus on application behavior.

solutions.name: Self-hosted inference
solutions.blurb: Runs model weights on controlled infrastructure for cost, privacy, latency, or customization reasons.

solutions.name: Batch inference
solutions.blurb: Runs model predictions over scheduled datasets when latency is less important than throughput and cost.

avoidWhen: The project is still exploratory and local-only
Deployment requirements are unknown
Security/observability is not ready
The model/tooling choice is still unknown
tradeoffs: Managed platforms reduce ops but add vendor constraints
Self-hosting gives control but increases operational load
Static hosting is simple but only fits frontend/static workloads
Managed model APIs reduce infrastructure burden; local or self-hosted inference improves control but adds operations work
scale: Static sites to distributed model-serving platforms
firstMove: Start with locality, latency, cost, privacy, and operational ownership; pick managed APIs or self-hosted inference after those constraints are known.

subcategory: Production-scale service, data, AI, and workflow architecture
description: Choose high-impact architecture patterns for common production situations before selecting specific stacks or libraries.

solutions.name: Modular monolith
solutions.blurb: Keeps one deployable unit with explicit internal boundaries; often the best default until scale or ownership requires distribution.

solutions.name: Microservices
solutions.blurb: Splits independently deployable services around ownership and scaling boundaries; requires strong observability and platform discipline.

solutions.name: Event-driven architecture
solutions.blurb: Decouples producers and consumers with events, queues, streams, idempotency, retries, ordering, and backpressure controls.

solutions.name: CQRS / Event sourcing
solutions.blurb: Separates write decisions from read models; event sourcing preserves a durable event log when auditability and replay matter.

solutions.name: Lakehouse / ELT architecture
solutions.blurb: Stores raw and curated data in warehouse/lakehouse layers with contracts, lineage, tests, and transformation orchestration.

solutions.name: Streaming analytics architecture
solutions.blurb: Uses streams, consumers, windows, state stores, and sinks for low-latency metrics, alerts, and operational data products.

solutions.name: RAG architecture
solutions.blurb: Combines ingestion, chunking, embeddings, retrieval, reranking, prompt assembly, generation, citation, and evaluation.

solutions.name: Model serving architecture
solutions.blurb: Chooses managed APIs, self-hosted inference, batching, caching, observability, safety filters, and rollout controls.

solutions.name: Reliability envelope
solutions.blurb: SLOs, error budgets, health checks, observability, canaries, rollback plans, and incident feedback loops.

solutions.name: Resilience patterns
solutions.blurb: Timeouts, retries with jitter, idempotency keys, circuit breakers, bulkheads, queues, and backpressure.

solutions.name: Data contract practices
solutions.blurb: Schema evolution, compatibility checks, ownership, lineage, and consumer-driven expectations for data and events.

bestFor: Choosing system shape before stack/library selection
avoidWhen: A single local script or one-off analysis solves the problem
The team has no operational ownership for the architecture chosen
tradeoffs: Architecture patterns improve repeatability but can add ceremony
Distributed designs increase coordination, observability, and failure-mode costs
scale: Single product teams to large distributed systems
firstMove: Start by naming the dominant constraint: ownership, scale, latency, reliability, data freshness, or model locality; choose architecture only after that constraint is explicit.

subcategory: CPU, SIMD, multicore, GPU, CUDA, ROCm, x86, ARM, and accelerator choices
description: Match compute-heavy workloads to hardware and parallelism models before optimizing code or choosing low-level libraries.

solutions.name: SIMD vectorization
solutions.blurb: Use when the same operation applies to contiguous data; best for numeric loops, image/audio kernels, parsing, compression, and vectorized scans.
solutions.timeComplexity: Same big-O as scalar code, lower constant factor by processing multiple lanes per instruction
solutions.spaceComplexity: Usually O(1) extra; alignment/padding may add small buffers

solutions.name: MIMD / multicore parallelism
solutions.blurb: Use when independent tasks or partitions can run on multiple cores; watch synchronization, contention, false sharing, and memory bandwidth.
solutions.timeComplexity: Ideal O(work / cores), limited by serial work, coordination, and bandwidth

solutions.name: x86 vs ARM CPU choice
solutions.blurb: x86 often has broad server/library maturity and AVX options; ARM often wins on power/cost density and NEON/SVE-supported workloads.

solutions.name: CUDA
solutions.blurb: Use for massively parallel numeric work, deep learning, simulation, and vector/matrix workloads when data transfer overhead is amortized.
solutions.timeComplexity: Same algorithmic big-O, but parallel throughput can reduce wall time for suitable kernels
solutions.spaceComplexity: Requires device memory for inputs, outputs, and intermediate tensors/buffers

solutions.name: ROCm / HIP
solutions.blurb: Use for AMD GPU acceleration where ROCm-supported hardware and libraries match the workload and deployment constraints.

solutions.name: GPU vs CPU decision
solutions.blurb: Prefer GPU for high arithmetic intensity and parallel kernels; prefer CPU when branching, latency, small batches, or transfer overhead dominates.

solutions.name: BLAS/LAPACK
solutions.blurb: Use vendor-optimized matrix/vector kernels instead of hand-rolling dense linear algebra.

solutions.name: Columnar/vectorized execution
solutions.blurb: Use for analytics scans and aggregations where batches, cache locality, compression, and SIMD-friendly execution dominate.

bestFor: Tight latency or cost targets after algorithmic wins are exhausted
avoidWhen: The workload is I/O-bound or small batch
The team cannot test on target hardware
A library already solves the bottleneck well
tradeoffs: Hardware acceleration improves throughput but adds portability and deployment constraints
GPU speedups can disappear if transfer overhead or branching dominates
scale: Single machine kernels to distributed accelerated clusters
firstMove: Start with profiling and data movement; use optimized libraries before writing SIMD, CUDA, or ROCm kernels.

subcategory: Language runtime, compiler, native extension, JIT, AOT, WASM, and portability choices
description: Choose runtimes and compiler strategies based on latency, throughput, safety, deployment, ecosystem, and team skill.

solutions.name: Python + native libraries
solutions.blurb: Best when productivity and data/AI ecosystem matter; push hot loops into NumPy, PyTorch, Rust, Cython, or compiled extensions.

solutions.name: JVM
solutions.blurb: Strong for long-running services, mature tooling, concurrency, GC tuning, and enterprise ecosystems.

solutions.name: Go
solutions.blurb: Good default for network services, CLIs, static binaries, simple concurrency, and operationally small deployments.

solutions.name: Rust
solutions.blurb: Use when memory safety, predictable performance, native integration, and low runtime overhead matter.

solutions.name: JIT vs AOT
solutions.blurb: JIT can optimize hot paths at runtime; AOT improves startup, deployment predictability, and constrained environments.

solutions.name: WebAssembly
solutions.blurb: Use for sandboxed portable modules in browsers, edge runtimes, plugins, or cross-language embedding.

solutions.name: Native extensions
solutions.blurb: Use when most app code benefits from a high-level language but hotspots need compiled speed or system-library access.

solutions.name: LLVM/Clang
solutions.blurb: Broad compiler backend and tooling ecosystem for native optimization, sanitizers, static analysis, and custom language work.

solutions.name: GCC
solutions.blurb: Mature native compiler suite with broad platform support and strong optimization for systems code.

bestFor: Balancing speed, safety, ecosystem, and operability
Deciding when to use native code or WASM
avoidWhen: A proven team standard already fits the workload
The decision is driven by novelty rather than constraints
tradeoffs: Runtime choice affects hiring, libraries, deployment, latency, memory, and observability
Compiler performance wins can increase build and portability complexity
scale: Scripts to production platforms and native systems
firstMove: Start with deployment, latency, safety, ecosystem, and team skill constraints; change runtime/compiler only for measured needs.

subcategory: Archival interview-style patterns and reusable problem classes
description: Distills many coding-challenge problems into reusable classes, while keeping trick-only variants archival unless they map to real production work.

solutions.name: Two pointers / sliding window
solutions.blurb: Useful for contiguous sequence scans, streaming windows, deduplication, and bounded-memory filters; many puzzle variants are archival.
solutions.timeComplexity: Usually O(n)
solutions.implementationNote: Hand-roll when the window invariant is domain-specific; prefer streaming/window libraries for production analytics pipelines.
solutions.code:
def longest_subarray_at_most_k(nums, k):
    left = 0
    total = 0
    best = 0

    for right, value in enumerate(nums):
        total += value
        while total > k and left <= right:
            total -= nums[left]
            left += 1
        best = max(best, right - left + 1)

    return best

solutions.name: Prefix sums / difference arrays
solutions.blurb: Useful for range queries, cumulative metrics, histograms, and batch interval updates.
solutions.timeComplexity: O(n) preprocessing; O(1) simple range sums or O(log n) with Fenwick tree
solutions.implementationNote: Hand-roll for simple range math; use database/window functions, pandas, NumPy, or OLAP engines when the data already lives there.

solutions.name: Heap / priority queue
solutions.blurb: Useful for scheduling, top-k, merge streams, shortest paths, retries, and priority work queues.
solutions.timeComplexity: O(log n) push/pop; O(1) peek
solutions.implementationNote: Use standard-library heaps or managed priority queues; hand-roll only for custom decrease-key, indexed heap, or domain-specific scheduling semantics.

solutions.name: Union-find
solutions.blurb: Useful for connectivity, grouping, Kruskal MST, image/component labeling, and equivalence classes.
solutions.timeComplexity: Near O(1) amortized with path compression and union by rank
solutions.implementationNote: Hand-roll for lightweight connectivity logic; use graph/image libraries when connectivity is part of a larger analytics or vision workflow.

solutions.name: Trick-only puzzle variants
solutions.blurb: Keep as archival when the pattern is mainly an interview trick and production code would use a database, library, solver, or clearer custom implementation.
solutions.implementationNote: Use for recognition and interview prep; do not promote to production design unless the trick maps directly to real constraints.

solutions.name: Library-replaced algorithms
solutions.blurb: Prefer maintained libraries for search, optimization, parsing, crypto, ML, and graph analytics unless custom constraints justify hand-rolling.
solutions.implementationNote: Prefer maintained libraries or platform primitives for search, solvers, parsers, crypto, ML, and graph analytics unless custom constraints dominate.

bestFor: Choosing hand-rolled code only when constraints justify it
Archiving puzzle variants without overemphasizing them
avoidWhen: A database query, library, solver, or platform primitive solves the real problem more reliably
The trick does not map to production constraints
tradeoffs: Pattern literacy helps implementation judgment but can overfit to puzzle framing
Production systems often need libraries, data modeling, and observability more than clever code
scale: Local implementation details to selected production hot paths
